Listening to Seattle’s birds with Convolutional Neural Networks¶

By Duong Le¶

https://github.com/th-duongle/chirp

In [73]:
import h5py
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import random
import seaborn as sns
import time
import pandas as pd
import librosa
import librosa.display

from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.optimizers import Adam
from tensorflow.keras.layers import BatchNormalization, GlobalAveragePooling2D, Conv2D, MaxPooling2D, Flatten, Dense, Dropout
from tensorflow.keras.callbacks import EarlyStopping
from tensorflow.keras.utils import to_categorical

from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix, roc_curve, auc
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder
from sklearn.utils import shuffle
from sklearn.utils.class_weight import compute_class_weight

Preprocessing¶

Preprocessing pipeline:

  • Load data
  • Exploratory data analysis (observe number of birds, number of samples, dimensions, summary statistics, sample spectrogram visualization)
  • Preprocess master dataset (transpose, normalize values, add CNN channel dimension)
  • Create binary subset and multi-class subset
In [2]:
f = h5py.File("bird_spectrograms.hdf5", "r")
In [3]:
species_keys = list(f.keys())
print("Number of bird species:", len(species_keys))
print(species_keys[:13])

Number of bird species: 12
['amecro', 'amerob', 'bewwre', 'bkcchi', 'daejun', 'houfin', 'houspa', 'norfli', 'rewbla', 'sonspa', 'spotow', 'whcspa']

Spectrogram is basically a picture of sound over time. When looking at a sample data shape (128, 517, 66):

  • 128 represents the height, which is the different pitch or frequency levels. Lower frequencies represent deeper sounds, and higher frequencies represent sharper chirps.
  • 517 represents the width, which is the number of small moments (or time slices) in time.
  • 66 is the number of recordings.

This corresponds with the storage format of (height, width, number of samples). When visually inspecting a spectrogram, the columns is when the sound happen, the rows is what pitch existed, and bright is how strong it was.

In [4]:
sample_species = species_keys[0]
print("Sample species:", sample_species)
print("Dataset shape:")
print(f[sample_species].shape)

Sample species: amecro
Dataset shape:
(128, 517, 66)
In [7]:
summary = []

for species in species_keys:

    dataset = f[species]

    summary.append({
        "Species": species,
        "Frequency level": dataset.shape[0],
        "Time slices": dataset.shape[1],
        "Number of recordings": dataset.shape[2]})

summary_df = pd.DataFrame(summary)

summary_df = summary_df.sort_values(by="Number of recordings",ascending=False)

summary_df.head(13)

In [8]:
# make sure dimensions are similar
unique_shapes = set()
for species in species_keys:
    shape = f[species].shape[:2]
    unique_shapes.add(shape)
print(unique_shapes)

{(128, 517)}

The decibel spectrograms are log-scaled. The loudest frequency regions are near 0 dB. Audio below -80 dB is clipped as anything quieter is basically negligible. The mean shows that most of the spectrogram is relatively quiet and bird chirps occupy only portions of the frequency space. Most pixels are background. The standard deviation shows a decent variation and contrast in the spectrogram, which is helpful for convolutional neural network learning. We definitely need to normalize so that values are on controlled scales, which will help neural networks to train better.

sample_data = f[sample_species][:]
print("Minimum:", sample_data.min())
print("Maximum:", sample_data.max())
print("Mean:", sample_data.mean())
print("Std:", sample_data.std())

# sample spectrogram
sample_spectrogram = f[sample_species][9]
plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
plt.imshow(sample_spectrogram,aspect='auto',origin='lower')
plt.title(f"Spectrogram of Song Sparrow")
plt.xlabel("Time")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.colorbar(label="Amplitude")
plt.show()

Dataset¶

  • Create a master data that is transposed and normalized
  • Create a binary subset with only two bird species
  • Create a multiclass subset with a cap 100 samples for each type of bird.
  • When final architecture is decided, the neural network will train with the full data

Master dataset¶

  • Transpose the data into the format that a CNN would expect
  • Normalize spectrogram values to improve convergence speed and gradient behavior (neural networks train more stably when inputs are on a consistent scale)
  • Add CNN channel dimension because CNNs expect (gray-scale) image-like input
In [11]:
X_all = []
y_all = []

for species in species_keys:
    data = f[species][:]
    data = np.transpose(data, (2, 0, 1))
    X_all.append(data)
    y_all.extend([species] * data.shape[0])

X_all = np.concatenate(X_all, axis=0)
y_all = np.array(y_all)

print("X_all shape:", X_all.shape)
print("y_all shape:", y_all.shape)

X_all shape: (1981, 128, 517)
y_all shape: (1981,)
In [12]:
X_all = X_all.astype("float32")
X_min = X_all.min()
X_max = X_all.max()
X_all = (X_all - X_min) / (X_max - X_min)
print(X_all.min(), X_all.max())

0.0 1.0
In [13]:
X_all = X_all[..., np.newaxis]
print(X_all.shape)

(1981, 128, 517, 1)

Binary subset¶

The binary subset will include the House Sparrow and the Song Sparrow.

In [14]:
binary_mask = np.isin(y_all,["houspa", "sonspa"])

X_binary = X_all[binary_mask]
y_binary = y_all[binary_mask]
In [15]:
print(X_binary.shape)
print(y_binary.shape)

(893, 128, 517, 1)
(893,)
In [16]:
encoder = LabelEncoder()
y_binary_encoded = encoder.fit_transform(y_binary)
print(encoder.classes_)

['houspa' 'sonspa']

Multiclass subset¶

  • Each bird should have 300 samples.
  • If they have more than 300, we are only keeping 300.
  • If they have less than 300, we use SpecAugment.
In [40]:
# data augmentation function
def spec_augment(spectrogram,freq_mask_param=10,time_mask_param=20):
    augmented = spectrogram.copy()

    # frequency mask
    freq_mask_size = np.random.randint(0, freq_mask_param)
    freq_start = np.random.randint(0,augmented.shape[0] - freq_mask_size)
    augmented[freq_start:freq_start + freq_mask_size,:] = 0


    # time mask
    time_mask_size = np.random.randint(0, time_mask_param)
    time_start = np.random.randint(0,augmented.shape[1] - time_mask_size)
    augmented[:,time_start:time_start + time_mask_size] = 0

    return augmented
In [41]:
X_multi = []
y_multi = []

target_samples = 300

for species in species_keys:
    print(f"\nProcessing species: {species}")
    species_mask = (y_all == species)
    X_species = X_all[species_mask]
    n_original = X_species.shape[0]
    print(f"Original samples: {n_original}")
    X_species_final = list(X_species)

    # augment only minority samples
    if n_original < target_samples:
        n_needed = target_samples - n_original
        print(f"Generating {n_needed} augmented samples")

        for i in range(n_needed):
            idx = np.random.randint(0, n_original)
            original_spec = X_species[idx]
            augmented_spec = spec_augment(original_spec)
            X_species_final.append(augmented_spec)
            
    else:
        selected_idx = np.random.choice(n_original,size=target_samples,replace=False)
        X_species_final = list(X_species[selected_idx])

    X_species_final = np.array(X_species_final)
    X_multi.append(X_species_final)
    y_multi.extend([species] * len(X_species_final))

Generating 216 augmented samples

Processing species: norfli

Processing species: sonspa

Binary Classification¶

In [34]:
# train test split
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X_binary,y_binary_encoded,test_size=0.2,stratify=y_binary_encoded,random_state=42)

print(X_train.shape)
print(X_test.shape)

(714, 128, 517, 1)
(179, 128, 517, 1)

First Binary Model¶

  • Spectrogram representation is working
  • CNN is able to learn meaningful acoustic patterns
  • CNN is able to distinguish between the two birds
  • The main issue is class imbalance and poorer minority class recall
  • The model misses 25% of Song Sparrow samples
  • Proposed improvement: don't make the model more complex. Add class weights to address imbalance.
In [35]:
# base architecture
baseline_model = Sequential([
    Conv2D(32,(3,3),activation='relu',input_shape=(128, 517, 1)),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),
    Conv2D(64,(3,3),activation='relu'),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),
    Flatten(),
    Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')])

To enable the following instructions: AVX2 FMA, in other operations, rebuild TensorFlow with the appropriate compiler flags.

  super().__init__(activity_regularizer=activity_regularizer, **kwargs)
In [36]:
baseline_model.compile(optimizer='adam',loss='binary_crossentropy',metrics=['accuracy'])

Second Binary Model¶

print(class_weights)

# second binary model
baseline_model = Sequential([
    Conv2D(32,(3,3),activation='relu',input_shape=(128, 517, 1)),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),
    Conv2D(64,(3,3),activation='relu'),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),
    Flatten(),
    Dense(64, activation='relu'),
    Dropout(0.5),
    Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')])

# early stop

In [57]:
start_time = time.time()
history = baseline_model.fit(X_train,y_train,epochs=30,batch_size=16,validation_split=0.2,class_weight=class_weights,callbacks=[early_stop],verbose=1)
end_time = time.time()
training_time = end_time - start_time
print(f"\nTotal Training Time: {training_time:.2f} seconds")

In [60]:
print(classification_report(y_test,preds,target_names=encoder.classes_))

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

      houspa       0.88      0.79      0.83       126
      sonspa       0.60      0.74      0.66        53

    accuracy                           0.78       179
   macro avg       0.74      0.76      0.75       179
weighted avg       0.80      0.78      0.78       179

Binary Evaluation and Comparison¶

The base model produces a strong test accuracy, showcasing ability to predict House Sparrow birds. Precision, recall and F-1 are important here; they are indicators of the class imbalance between the two birds. While accuracy was high, Song Sparrow performance was poorer, specifically in recall and F-1. The model occasionaly failed to identify true Song Sparrow recordings.

The second model has a similar architecture, but with balanced class weights, dropout and early stopping. This proved not useful. The revised model produced lower performance. Precision and F-1 of Song Sparrow decreased significantly; this means that the model became overly sensitive to minority class predictions and introduced more false positives.

Parameters First Model Second Model
Number of Convolutional Layers 2 2
Number of Pooling Layers 2 2
Number of Flatten Layers 1 1
Number of Dense Layers 2 2
Number of Dropout Layers 0 1
Class Weights Not applied Balanced
Early Stopping Not applied Yes
Accuracy 0.88 0.78
Preferred Model Yes No
Results First Model Second Model
Precision (House Sparrow) 0.90 0.88
Precision (Song Sparrow) 0.82 0.60
Recall (House Sparrow) 0.93 0.79
Recall (Song Sparrow) 0.75 0.74
F-1 Score (House Sparrow) 0.91 0.83
F-1 Score (Song Sparrow) 0.78 0.66

Multiclass Classification Model¶

In [44]:
# encode lables
multi_encoder = LabelEncoder()
y_multi_encoded = multi_encoder.fit_transform(y_multi)
print(multi_encoder.classes_)
print("Number of classes:", len(multi_encoder.classes_))

['amecro' 'amerob' 'bewwre' 'bkcchi' 'daejun' 'houfin' 'houspa' 'norfli'
 'rewbla' 'sonspa' 'spotow' 'whcspa']
Number of classes: 12
In [45]:
# train test split
X_train_multi, X_test_multi, y_train_multi, y_test_multi = train_test_split(X_multi,y_multi_encoded,test_size=0.2,stratify=y_multi_encoded,random_state=42)
print(X_train_multi.shape)
print(X_test_multi.shape)

(2880, 128, 517, 1)
(720, 128, 517, 1)
In [46]:
# one hot encode
num_classes = len(np.unique(y_multi_encoded))
y_train_multi_cat = to_categorical(y_train_multi,num_classes=num_classes)
y_test_multi_cat = to_categorical(y_test_multi,num_classes=num_classes)

First Multiclass Model¶

In [47]:
# base architecture
multi_model1 = Sequential([
    Conv2D(32,(3,3),activation='relu',input_shape=(128, 517, 1)),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),
    Conv2D(64,(3,3),activation='relu'),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),
    Flatten(),
    Dense(128, activation='relu'),
    Dense(num_classes, activation='softmax')])

/Users/thuyduongle/anaconda3/lib/python3.11/site-packages/keras/src/layers/convolutional/base_conv.py:107: UserWarning: Do not pass an `input_shape`/`input_dim` argument to a layer. When using Sequential models, prefer using an `Input(shape)` object as the first layer in the model instead.
  super().__init__(activity_regularizer=activity_regularizer, **kwargs)

multi_model1.summary()

Second Multiclass Model¶

In [57]:
# architecture
multi_model2 = Sequential([
    Conv2D(32,(3,3),activation='relu',padding='same',input_shape=(128, 517, 1)),
    BatchNormalization(),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),
    Conv2D(64,(3,3),activation='relu',padding='same'),
    BatchNormalization(),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),
    Conv2D(128,(3,3),activation='relu',padding='same'),
    BatchNormalization(),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),
    GlobalAveragePooling2D(),
    Dense(128, activation='relu'),
    Dropout(0.4),
    Dense(num_classes, activation='softmax')])

# compile
multi_model2.compile(optimizer=Adam(learning_rate=0.0003),
                     loss='categorical_crossentropy',
                     metrics=['accuracy'])
multi_model2.summary()


# early stopping
early_stop_multi2 = EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss',patience=5,restore_best_weights=True)

Prediction Using 3 Audios¶

  • Preprocess audio function:
    • load audio
    • keep first 3 seconds
    • create spectrograms
    • resize dimensions
    • add cnn dimensions
In [78]:
def preprocess_audio(file_path):
    audio, sr = librosa.load(file_path,sr=22050)
    audio = audio[:3 * sr]

    spectrogram = librosa.feature.melspectrogram(y=audio,sr=sr,n_mels=128)
    spectrogram_db = librosa.power_to_db(
        spectrogram,
        ref=np.max)

   
    spectrogram_db = (spectrogram_db - spectrogram_db.min()) / (spectrogram_db.max() - spectrogram_db.min())

    target_width = 517
    current_width = spectrogram_db.shape[1]
    if current_width < target_width:
        pad_width = target_width - current_width
        spectrogram_db = np.pad(
            spectrogram_db,
            ((0,0),(0,pad_width)),
            mode='constant')
    else:
        spectrogram_db = spectrogram_db[:, :target_width]
        
    spectrogram_db = spectrogram_db.reshape(1,128,517,1)
    
    return spectrogram_db
In [76]:
def plot_spectrogram(spec, title):
    plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
    plt.imshow(
        spec[0,:,:,0],
        aspect='auto',
        origin='lower',
        cmap='magma')
    plt.title(title)
    plt.xlabel("Time")
    plt.ylabel("Frequency")
    plt.colorbar(label='Normalized Intensity')
    plt.show()
In [90]:
test_files = ["test1.mp3","test2.mp3","test3.mp3"]

for file in test_files:
    spec = preprocess_audio(file)
    plot_spectrogram(spec, file)

    probs = multi_model2.predict(spec)[0]
    top_idx = np.argmax(probs)
    predicted_species = multi_encoder.classes_[top_idx]
    print(f"\nTop Prediction: {predicted_species}")

    # probabilities
    sorted_idx = np.argsort(probs)[::-1]
    print("\nTop 5 Probabilities:")
    
    for idx in sorted_idx[:5]:
        species = multi_encoder.classes_[idx]
        probability = probs[idx]
        print(f"{species}: {probability:.4f}")
